gist: Blockworks analysts (Carlos, Toma, Ian Answorth of Cairo Street) argue SOL captures little of the value applications generate on Solana, and dissect new SIMD proposals (SIMD-547/553 compute-unit burn, Helius disinflation acceleration) as a fix gated on the still-undated Alpenglow upgrade. They also weigh AI models (fictional 'Claude Fable'/'Mythos') raising DeFi smart-contract exploit risk, and MicroStrategy/Saylor's growing structural overhang on Bitcoin and the broader crypto market.

themes.theme: SOL value accrual and SIMD proposals

insights.claim: Solana applications have become highly profitable while SOL the asset has not benefited proportionally: the ratio of application revenue to REV is roughly 5x today versus about 1x in 2023, understating the leakage because not all REV accrues to token holders.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the core bear case on L1 value accrual; a mechanism that recaptures app-level value would be a structural re-rating catalyst for SOL.
insights.quote: the ratio of application revenue to REB is like 5x today up from like 1x at some point 19 2023

insights.claim: SIMD-547 (now formalized as SIMD-553), proposed by KB of Temporal, restores an activity-linked SOL burn via a new fee tied to requested compute units, set at 0.5 lamports per CU (up from an initial 0.1).
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: A CU-based burn is denomination-agnostic, so it captures value even as apps move fees to USDC; watch the GitHub proposal and lamport parameter as the key SOL tokenomics variable.

insights.claim: At current usage the SIMD-553 CU burn would destroy roughly 7,000 to 9,000 SOL per day (about $190M annualized at ~8,000/day), offsetting around 12% of daily SOL issuance but not making SOL disinflationary.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Sizes the actual impact: meaningful offset but not net deflationary, tempering expectations of a large mechanical bid for SOL.
insights.quote: If you assume like 8,000 sole burn uh based on current estimates that that's around $190 million in burn

insights.claim: The key advantage of the CU-based burn is that it scales with both network usage and network capacity, so as Solana raises block limits and lowers slot times the burn rises roughly linearly.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Aligns Solana's scaling roadmap (more CUs, faster blocks) with SOL economic value, turning throughput growth into token sink growth.

insights.claim: The CU burn can only be safely implemented after the Alpenglow upgrade because doing it earlier would sharply raise validator voting costs and disadvantage smaller validators, and Alpenglow has no live timeline.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Execution risk is the binding constraint; SOL value-accrual fixes may be 18 months out, so the thesis is theoretical until Alpenglow ships.
insights.quote: the main concern for me is to actually the execution of this

insights.claim: Helius has proposed doubling Solana's disinflation rate from 15% to 30% so the network reaches its ~1.5% terminal inflation in roughly 3 years instead of 6, versus current ~3.8% annualized inflation.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Faster disinflation attacks SOL value accrual from the issuance side; combined with the burn it materially changes net supply growth math for SOL holders.

insights.claim: Prior to SIMD-96 (Feb 2025), 50% of Solana priority fees were burned and 50% went to validators, but SIMD-96 removed the burn to curb out-of-protocol transaction-inclusion deals, leaving SOL today reliant almost entirely on issuance for staking yield.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Explains the structural origin of SOL's weak value capture; the new SIMDs are a direct attempt to reverse an unintended consequence of SIMD-96.

insights.claim: Despite SOL making an all-time high near $260 in January, its market cap was already nearly double the 2021 cycle, illustrating how inflation diluted price appreciation.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Frames SOL's inflation drag in market-cap terms: holders needed far more capital inflow to reach the same nominal price, a key dilution lens for valuation.
insights.quote: sole made an all-time high of 260 or something like in January, but the market cap was already like almost double what it was like back in 2021

insights.claim: Pump Fun alone accounts for over 20% of compute-unit usage on Solana, making it the largest single source of value the network currently fails to capture.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Concentration means a CU burn would disproportionately capture value from a single app; also a risk if Pump Fun migrates fees or volume away.
insights.quote: Pump Fun uh currently accounts for over 20% of compute unit usage on Solana

insights.claim: Applications are increasingly denominating fees in USDC rather than SOL, with Pump Fun adding USDC bonding (implying a Circle side deal), 0x's Edge product going stablecoin-denominated, and Jito's JTX pairs likely USDC, creating a risk that SOL gets bypassed even as the chain grows.
insights.speaker: Ian Answorth (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Stablecoin-denominated app fees threaten SOL demand; the CU-based burn is attractive precisely because it is denomination-agnostic and sidesteps this leakage.

insights.claim: A CU-priced fee would trigger a reaction function where protocols optimize code to cut requested CUs, but more efficient block packing simply lets more transactions fill blocks, so price-sensitive demand and total burn largely persist.
insights.speaker: Ian Answorth (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Counters the worry that CU optimization neuters the burn; induced demand backfills blocks, supporting durable burn levels.

insights.claim: Evidence that crypto users are price-insensitive: Benedict Brady's research showed Axiom traders being charged ~100x the actual Solana transaction fee yet continuing to trade there, supporting Solana capturing that value rather than ceding it to apps.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: If demand is fee-inelastic, the network can extract more value via the burn without losing activity, strengthening the SOL accrual case.
insights.quote: traders on Axiom being charged like 100 X the actual Solana transaction fee and they still trading on Axiom

insights.claim: Brandon Watt of Anza broadly supported SIMD-553 on GitHub but flagged that 0.5 lamports/CU may be too aggressive a starting parameter, suggesting 0.1-0.2 to avoid harming high-frequency traders and market makers.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Core-team support signals the proposal is viable; the lamport parameter is the live negotiation variable that determines how much SOL actually burns.

insights.claim: Networks cannot easily extract Apple-style app-store rents on open-source chains because apps can migrate to other chains and abstract it from users, so value capture must be protocol-native (like the CU burn) rather than a middleman fee.
insights.speaker: Danny (host) / Ian Answorth
insights.so_what: Explains why CU-based burns, not app taxes, are the realistic L1 monetization path; relevant to valuing any L1 token's long-run cash-flow potential.

themes.theme: AI models and DeFi smart-contract risk

insights.claim: Anthropic's newly released 'Claude Fable' is a restricted version of the unreleased 'Mythos' model that refuses certain tasks (e.g. developing frontier models, cybersecurity work) and silently downgrades to a prior model on flagged requests.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: Capability gating to whitelisted enterprises limits but does not eliminate exploit-tooling proliferation risk for open-source crypto code.

insights.claim: Newer AI models are markedly better at one-shotting novel tasks including auditing and hardening smart-contract code, with crypto developers and Toly (via a 'percolator' side project) reporting Fable found and fixed bugs prior models missed.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: AI-assisted auditing should become standard practice; protocols that fail to continuously re-audit against new models face rising exploit risk.

insights.claim: AI is being used to find real exploits in crypto, with the Zcash bug attributed to 'Opus 48' and stale low-usage protocols like Ambient Finance recently hacked, with attackers concentrating on dormant capital especially on Ethereum.
insights.speaker: Carlos / Danny
insights.so_what: Stale TVL on older chains is a growing attack target; capital should rotate toward actively maintained, battle-tested protocols.
insights.quote: even that Zcash bug that was found was like Opus 48, right?

insights.claim: Heightened AI exploit capability will widen dispersion in DeFi, concentrating deposit flows into a few conservative, never-exploited, well-resourced 'A-tier' protocols and reinforcing the existing lending-market winner-take-most dynamic.
insights.speaker: Ian Answorth (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Trade the flight to quality: blue-chip lending/DeFi names should outperform long-tail protocols as security premia rise.

insights.claim: Per Tarun on The Chopping Block, attacker-vs-defender dynamics have a tipping point where each exploit on a random contract hardens the rest, so the industry emerges more resilient after a painful adjustment.
insights.speaker: Toma (Blockworks advisory)
insights.so_what: Frames near-term hack risk as a transient cost preceding greater systemic resilience; relevant to timing DeFi exposure through the volatility.
insights.quote: every like attack on a random smart contract hardens the rest

insights.claim: Wallets (e.g. Phantom) are framed as the highest-impact attack surface because they are the gateway to many protocols, making a wallet-level compromise far worse than hacking an obscure DeFi protocol.
insights.speaker: Ian Answorth (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Wallet security is the systemic single point of failure; infrastructure-level wallet risk deserves more weight than individual protocol risk.

insights.claim: Much DeFi yield has historically been fake yield from untracked token emissions subsidizing returns, and the market is shifting toward real yield from genuine risk-taking and real-asset issuers (e.g. Athena/Ethena) and large asset managers launching products directly on chain rather than via existing DeFi protocols.
insights.speaker: Ian Answorth (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Distinguish emissions-driven from cash-flow yield when valuing protocols; real-yield issuers bypassing DeFi as a distribution channel pressures incumbent protocol economics.

insights.claim: Saylor's Stretch yield product grew large enough that a high single-digit percent of all Stretch was tokenized on chain, and after he sold ~32 BTC to fund interest payments the market feared forced Bitcoin sales before Strategy instead bought more BTC by selling MSTR via its ATM.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: Reveals Saylor's trilemma; in a worst case he sacrifices MSTR common shareholders to keep Stretch funded, a key tail risk for MSTR equity holders.

insights.claim: Bitcoin's bid is now structurally narrow: Saylor and leveraged dip-buyers are the marginal buyers while spot Bitcoin ETFs have seen over $5B of outflows across roughly the last 5 weeks, signaling institutional caution.
insights.speaker: Carlos / Toma
insights.so_what: Persistent ETF outflows plus a Saylor-dependent bid is a fragile market structure; watch ETF flows as the cleaner institutional sentiment gauge.
insights.quote: ETF outflows over the past uh 5 weeks, like consecutive outflows. I think it's about over 5 billion dollars

insights.claim: Bitcoin's original first-principles thesis (non-sovereign money) has become circular: the dominant market thesis is now simply that Saylor is buying, so the asset's bid depends on continued Saylor flows rather than fundamentals.
insights.speaker: Toma (Blockworks advisory)
insights.so_what: If Saylor's accumulation stalls, the reflexive bid unwinds; this reframes BTC's marginal demand as a single-actor dependency, not a fundamental store-of-value flow.
insights.quote: what started as like uh first principles or fundamentals driven thesis is now a thesis on the basis that Saylor is buying. So, it becomes like kind of circular

insights.claim: Bitcoin's realized price sits near $53,000 with spot around $60,000, and historically BTC has bottomed at or below realized price, suggesting consolidation toward the low-$50s if drawdowns continue.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete on-chain support level (~$53K realized price) to watch as a potential cycle bottom zone.
insights.quote: if you look for instance at the realized price, it's sitting at like $53,000. Bitcoin is just at like 60 right now

insights.claim: Strategy/MicroStrategy now holds about 883,000 BTC, approaching one million, with an 'accumulate at any cost' posture that makes its actions a material downstream driver of all crypto asset prices.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: The sheer size means a change in Strategy's behavior could cascade across the whole asset class, warranting attention even from those uninterested in MSTR.
insights.quote: they own 900,000 BTC, so coming up on a million. I think it's 883,000 BTC

insights.claim: If Stretch stays de-pegged below $100 its dividend obligation rises, and panelists believe Saylor currently has the cash to pay but would sacrifice Strategy common shares to keep Stretch solvent in a worst case.
insights.speaker: Carlos (Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: MSTR equity is the shock absorber for Stretch's liabilities; a prolonged de-peg shifts risk onto common shareholders.

themes.theme: Crypto market structure and altcoin decorrelation

insights.claim: A select set of revenue-generating crypto assets has decorrelated from Bitcoin this year (reversing 2024 when most funds underperformed BTC), though panelists doubt this can persist in USD terms if Bitcoin keeps underperforming equities and macro.
insights.speaker: Ian Answorth (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Cites Austin Barrack's 'not the market to hide in BTC' view; favors fundamentally sound, cash-flowing tokens but warns USD-denominated outperformance is capped by overall market direction.
insights.quote: Austin Barrack had a tweet like maybe 3 weeks ago saying that this isn't the market to be hiding uh in BTC

insights.claim: Even fundamentally sound crypto businesses with real cash flows remain levered to trading activity, so a broad BTC-led sell-off would still hit their revenue estimates because BTC is the most-traded asset by volume.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: Revenue-based valuations of trading-fee protocols are not a safe haven in a downturn; model volume sensitivity when valuing exchange/DEX tokens.

insights.claim: Improving regulatory clarity is enabling more fundamentally sound tokens that pass cash flow back to holders, eroding the old regime where Bitcoin was the default 'hide' asset that fell least in drawdowns.
insights.speaker: Toma (Blockworks advisory)
insights.so_what: Structural shift toward RWAs and tokenized equities could reduce BTC's relative-safety premium over time, favoring cash-flow tokens.

insights.claim: Crypto protocol valuations are broadly compressing due to weaker demand and an oversupply of unwanted new tokens, though panelists do not see egregious private valuations for new yield protocols, which must prove themselves by 'walking through the fire'.
insights.speaker: Ian Answorth (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Token oversupply plus security-risk discounts argue for selectivity; new yield protocols face a higher bar and survivorship is the filter for premium re-rating.

insights.claim: Investors are taking concrete behavioral precautions on chain: avoiding unknown-team protocols, diversifying across vaults, and treating sub-risk-free ~9% vault yields as compensation for real, often underpriced, tail risk.
insights.speaker: Danny / Carlos
insights.so_what: Reframes how to price left-tail DeFi risk post-Avi-Kael drama; reputation and conservative risk management become a durable competitive moat for top protocols.
insights.quote: you're taking a lot of risk to be earning beyond, you know, the risk-free rate
